channel.title: Dennis Johan Loevlie
channel.description: Project pages — one page per paper. Pointer-rich index linked from the CV.

item.title: Synthetic Data Reveals Generalization Gaps in Correlated Multiple Instance Learning
item.dc:creator: Ethan Harvey
Dennis Johan Loevlie
Michael C. Hughes
item.description:
Paper arXiv BibTeX

TL;DR. Multiple-instance learning architectures that explicitly model inter-instance correlations underperform simpler non-correlated baselines on held-out distributions when correlations were learned rather than imposed by the architecture. We expose the gap with a synthetic benchmark whose Bayes-optimal classifier we can compute exactly.

Abstract

Multiple instance learning (MIL) is the standard formulation when bag-level labels gate instance-level features — pathology slides, ECG records, drug interaction graphs. Recent attention- and transformer-based MIL methods explicitly model inter-instance correlations. We construct a synthetic benchmark in which the Bayes-optimal classifier is analytically known and show that correlated MIL architectures generalize worse than non-correlated baselines despite higher in-distribution accuracy.

Why it matters

The result inverts a common assumption — that explicit correlation modeling is always at least neutral. We localize the failure to a specific class of distribution shifts, give a closed-form description of the gap on the synthetic family, and show that the same pattern reproduces on three real-world MIL benchmarks (Camelyon16, MUSK1, MUSK2).

Cite this work

Copy BibTeX 
@inproceedings{harvey2025synthetic,
  author    = {Harvey, Ethan and Loevlie, Dennis Johan and Hughes, Michael C.},
  title     = {Synthetic Data Reveals Generalization Gaps in Correlated Multiple Instance Learning},
  booktitle = {ML4H 2025 Symposium (Findings Track)},
  year      = {2025},
  url       = {https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.25759}
}
